coaching.coaching: Pin down dependency-order traversal mechanism after rebar ready with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: dependency-order traversal mechanism after rebar ready

coaching.coaching: Test the riskiest assumption behind rebar ready scope flag requirement first.
coaching.subject: rebar ready scope flag requirement

coaching.coaching: Plan how machine-verifiable AC verification method will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: machine-verifiable AC verification method

coaching.coaching: State the concrete attestation evidence the [operator-attested] out-of-codebase rationale attestation evidence will require (a change id / vote outcome / timestamp), recorded on the ticket.
coaching.subject: out-of-codebase rationale attestation evidence

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `rebar next-batch <epic>` alongside `rebar ready` in the Approach and ACs — matching the paired usage in rebar-implement/SKILL.md (line 211) and AGENTS.md (lines 96–101) — so the brainstorm agent has the conflict-aware, dependency-ordered batch selector that actually computes review order within the unblocked set.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'runs plan-review ONLY on unblocked tickets (identified by `rebar ready`), in dependency order'
rebar-implement/SKILL.md line 211: '`rebar ready --epic <id>` / `rebar next-batch <epic>` to get the unblocked, conflict-aware set'
AGENTS.md lines 96–101: '`next-batch <epic>` returns a conflict-aware unblocked batch... prefer `next-batch` over ad-hoc parallel review'
docs/plan-review-gate.md line 444: '`next-batch` returns a conflict-aware, dependency-ready batch; prefer it (and plain dependency order) over ad-hoc parallel review of related tickets'
src/rebar/_guides/writing-a-passing-plan.md line 42: '`next-batch` (dependency-ready, conflict-aware) over ad-hoc parallel review of related tickets'
ABSENCE: neither 'next-batch' nor conflict-aware ordering appears anywhere in the plan's Approach or ACs
findings.finding: The plan prescribes that the new SKILL.md section instruct the brainstorm agent to review unblocked tickets 'in dependency order' but names only `rebar ready` as the identification tool — without mentioning `rebar next-batch <epic>`, the conflict-aware, dependency-ordered batch selector that actually computes that ordering. `rebar ready` returns any unblocked ticket but does not guarantee or express dependency order within a batch of simultaneously-unblocked tickets. rebar-implement/SKILL.md (line 211), AGENTS.md (lines 96–101), and docs/plan-review-gate.md (line 444) all pair `rebar ready --epic <id>` with `rebar next-batch <epic>` for this purpose; the plan's Approach and ACs omit `next-batch` entirely, leaving the brainstorm agent with an underspecified ordering protocol.
findings.location: Approach section ('then runs plan-review ONLY on unblocked tickets (identified by `rebar ready`), in dependency order') and AC 1 ('verified by reading the edited section (grep shows "unblocked" and "dependency order" in the section)')
scenarios: The brainstorm agent, following the rewritten SKILL.md, calls `rebar ready` and receives multiple simultaneously-unblocked child tickets with no ordering signal; it reviews them in an arbitrary order rather than the dependency-graph order the plan intends, potentially triggering the invalidation path documented in plan-review-gate.md §'Review dependencies FIRST'.

An agent interprets 'in dependency order' as meaning `rebar ready` itself computes this order (it does not), and proceeds with a parallel or arbitrary review, causing subsequent dependency-invalidated attestations.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach, replace 'identified by `rebar ready`' with 'identified by `rebar ready --epic <id>` / `rebar next-batch <epic>` (conflict-aware, dependency-ordered batch)'. Update AC 1's grep check to include 'next-batch' alongside 'unblocked' and 'dependency order'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the rewritten SKILL.md section (and in the corresponding AC), replace bare `rebar ready` with `rebar ready --epic <epic-id>` (or `rebar next-batch <epic-id>`) so the unblocked set is scoped to the newly-created children, matching the pattern already used in rebar-implement/SKILL.md Phase 3.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'then runs plan-review ONLY on unblocked tickets (identified by `rebar ready`)'
rebar-implement/SKILL.md Phase 3: 'use `rebar ready --epic <id>` / `rebar next-batch <epic>` to get the unblocked, conflict-aware set' — already scoped to the epic
docs/cli-reference.md line 293: 'rebar ready [--output text|llm|json] [--epic=<id>] ...' — the --epic flag exists and scopes output to one epic's children
AGENTS.md lines 97–101: 'next-batch <epic>' and 'rebar ready' usage in parallel-agent workflow implies scoping to an epic is standard
ABSENCE: neither the Approach nor the ACs mention '--epic <id>' or 'rebar ready --epic' to scope the ready-set to the brainstorm's own newly-created children
findings.finding: The Approach section and ACs specify 'rebar ready' to identify unblocked tickets but do not mention the '--epic <id>' scope flag, so the edited SKILL.md section could instruct the brainstorm agent to operate on the global unblocked set rather than only the newly-created epic's children — potentially reviewing unrelated tickets from other epics while missing in-scope children that are not yet ready.
findings.location: Approach section (paragraph beginning 'Rewrite the…section…') and AC line 1 ('runs plan-review ONLY on unblocked tickets (identified by `rebar ready`)')
scenarios: Author runs a brainstorm that creates 5 child tickets for a new epic; in a project with 20 other open epics, 'rebar ready' returns 15 unrelated tickets from other epics that happen to be unblocked, while the brainstorm's own tickets may all be blocked by each other — the author reviews the wrong set and misses the intended ones.
The edited SKILL.md is used in a project with a single epic (the common getting-started case); the missing scope flag has no visible effect, masking the gap during initial validation.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach rewrite instruction and in AC line 1, change 'rebar ready' to 'rebar ready --epic <epic-id>' (the epic whose children were just created). Mirror rebar-implement's phrasing: 'use `rebar ready --epic <epic-id>` / `rebar next-batch <epic-id>` to get the unblocked, dependency-ordered set of the newly-created children'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Qualify `rebar ready` with `--epic <id>` in both the Approach narrative and in AC 2's description, so the rewritten SKILL.md section scopes the unblocked-set query to the brainstorm epic's children rather than the whole project.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'runs plan-review ONLY on unblocked tickets (identified by `rebar ready`)'
AC 2: 'names `rebar ready` for identifying the unblocked set'
cli-reference.md line 293: 'Usage: rebar ready [--output text|llm|json] [--epic=<id>] [--sort=<key>] [--no-pull]' — the --epic flag scopes to an epic's children
rebar-implement/SKILL.md line 211: '`rebar ready --epic <id>`' — always uses the scoping flag

Cached plan-review evidence: '.rebar/cache/plan-review/4819-5f75-d931-4b44/b4b704a1e7a11f3d0da8.json': 'The Approach section and ACs specify rebar ready to identify unblocked tickets but do not mention the --epic <id> scope flag'
findings.finding: The plan names `rebar ready` without the `--epic <id>` scope flag throughout the Approach and ACs. Without `--epic`, `rebar ready` returns all project-wide unblocked tickets, not those scoped to the children of the just-created brainstorm epic. This is an instruction-locality gap: the agent surface (the rewritten SKILL.md section) would direct the brainstorm agent to call an unscoped command that returns irrelevant tickets from other epics, making the unblocked-set identification protocol incorrect for the brainstorm context. A prior cached plan-review run on this ticket (`.rebar/cache/plan-review/4819-5f75-d931-4b44/b4b704a1e7a11f3d0da8.json`) independently identified this same gap.
findings.location: Approach section ('identified by `rebar ready`') and AC 2 ('names `rebar ready` for identifying the unblocked set')
scenarios: A brainstorm agent following the rewritten SKILL.md calls bare `rebar ready`, receives a list of unblocked tickets from across the entire project (other epics, other authors' work), and attempts to run `rebar review-plan` on tickets it did not create, wasting LLM review budget and reviewing irrelevant plans.

The fallback logic (empty `rebar ready` → diagnose cycle) fires incorrectly when the brainstorm epic's children are all blocked but other unrelated tickets exist — the unscoped call returns results, masking the real cycle.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace every bare `rebar ready` reference in the Approach and ACs with `rebar ready --epic <id>` (matching rebar-implement/SKILL.md line 211 and cli-reference.md's documented `--epic=<id>` flag).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'verified by reading the section' in AC2 and AC3 with concrete grep commands that anchor the required strings (e.g. `grep -E 'exit 2|INDETERMINATE' examples/agent-skills/rebar-brainstorm/SKILL.md` for AC2 and `grep -E 'rebar ready' ...` for the `rebar ready` reference; `grep -E 'cycle|empty' ...` for the fallback in AC3).
evidence: AC2: 'verified by reading the section'
AC3: 'verified by reading the section'
AC1 by contrast at least names the grep strings ('unblocked' and 'dependency order'), giving a reproducible check
findings.finding: ACs 2 and 3 state 'verified by reading the section' as their proving method, which is a prose-level effort check rather than a concrete, machine-verifiable command. An agent has no deterministic artifact to assert against — 'reading' is not reproducible evidence of the named content (exit 2 / INDETERMINATE, `rebar ready`, and the empty-set fallback) actually being present in the edited file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('The section references the no-LLM fast-fail...') and AC3 ('The section specifies a fallback...')
scenarios: An agent marks ACs 2 and 3 complete by a subjective read without confirming the specific required strings are present, producing a false-positive completion verdict.
A reviewer cannot reproduce the check — 'reading the section' yields different results for different readers.
findings.suggested_fix:
AC2: add `— verified by grep -E 'exit.2|INDETERMINATE|rebar ready' examples/agent-skills/rebar-brainstorm/SKILL.md showing matches in the edited section`.
AC3: add `— verified by grep -E 'empty|cycle|authoring error' examples/agent-skills/rebar-brainstorm/SKILL.md showing matches in the edited section`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the ticket-comment sub-criterion from AC4 into its own AC (or prepend `[operator-attested]` to the compound AC4) so the F1 operator-attested rule applies and the in-session verifiability of the external evidence is unambiguous.
evidence: AC4 text: 'the no-edit assessment rationale is recorded as a comment on this ticket'
AC5 by contrast is correctly tagged '[operator-attested]' for its similarly external evidence (Gerrit change number / votes)

F1 OPERATOR-ATTESTED RULE: 'classify a criterion whose checkbox text begins with the exact case-insensitive tag [operator-attested] as in-session completable when its done evidence inherently lives OUTSIDE the codebase'
findings.finding: AC4's second sub-criterion — 'the no-edit assessment rationale is recorded as a comment on this ticket' — requires evidence that lives outside the codebase (a ticket comment), but it lacks the `[operator-attested]` tag. Without the tag, the F1 OPERATOR-ATTESTED RULE does not apply, leaving the criterion's in-session verifiability ambiguous for an executing agent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('rebar-implement/SKILL.md is left byte-for-byte unchanged ... and the no-edit assessment rationale is recorded as a comment on this ticket')
scenarios: An agent attempts to verify the ticket-comment requirement via a deterministic command and finds no such command exists, causing confusion about whether the AC is satisfied.
The ticket-comment sub-criterion is treated as codebase-verifiable and silently skipped when no grep or diff covers it.
findings.suggested_fix: Prepend `[operator-attested]` to the second clause of AC4, or extract it as a standalone AC5-style criterion: `- [ ] [operator-attested] The no-edit assessment rationale for rebar-implement/SKILL.md is recorded as a comment on this ticket before close.`

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
